elements.sent-2411-001: We study the long-time behaviour of matrix-valued stochastic exponentials of Lévy processes, i.e. of multiplicative Lévy processes in the general linear group.
elements.sent-2411-002: In particular, we prove laws of large numbers as well as central limit theorems for the logarithmised norm, logarithmised entries and the logarithmised determinant of the stochastic exponential.
elements.sent-2411-003: Where possible, also Berry-Esseen bounds are stated.
elements.sent-2411-004: Let $L=(L_t)_{t\geq 0}$ be a Lévy process in $\RR^{d\times d}$.
elements.sent-2411-005: We are interested in the long time behaviour of the stochastic exponential of $L$, that is of the unique, càdlàg solution $X=(X_t)_{t\geq 0}$ in $\RR^{d\times d}$ of the stochastic differential equation (SDE) \begin{equation}\label{SDE} dX_t=X_{t-} dL_t,\; t\geq 0, \quad X_0=\Id,\end{equation} with $\Id$ the identity matrix.

elements.section-2411-003: Limit theorems for norm, entries and the one-point motion
elements.sent-2411-112: Throughout this section let $L=(L_t)_{t\geq 0}$ be a Lévy process in $\RR^{d\times d}$ that fulfils \eqref{eq-nonsingular , and let $X=(X_t)_{t\geq 0}$ denote its stochastic exponential.}
elements.sent-2411-113: In this section, we prove a strong law of large numbers and a central limit theorem with Berry-Esseen bounds for the logarithm of both the norm and the entries of the left L\'evy process $X$ in $\GL$.
elements.sent-2411-114: We will make use of corresponding limit theorems for (discrete-time) products of random matrices in $\GL$ as provided in \cite{Benoist2016,Xiao2022,Cuny2023 .}
elements.sent-2411-115: These limit theorems will require, in addition to natural moment assumptions, some geometric assumptions like irreducibility of the action of $X$ on $\R^d$, which we are going to study in the first part of this section.
elements.sent-2411-116: Note that in most of the literature on products of random matrices that we quote, e.g. \cite{Guivarch2016,Benoist2016,Grama2022 , products with left increments are considered, that is, products of the form \begin{equation}X_{n,n-1} \cdots X_{1}.\end{equation}.}
elements.sent-2411-117: Obviously, by taking the transpose, we can reverse the order of multiplication, and transfer results for left products to results for right products, and vice versa.
elements.sent-2411-118: In this subsection, we formulate the geometric assumptions on $X$ and show how they can be checked directly on the driving L\'evy process $L$.
elements.sent-2411-119: These assumptions will be formulated in terms of the group $G_X$ generated by (the support) of $X$.
elements.sent-2411-120: The group $G_X$ equals the group generated by \begin{equation}\bigcup_{t >0} \supp(\mu_t).\end{equation}.

elements.sent-2411-122: We will impose the following irreducibility and proximality conditions on $G_X$, cf.. \cite{Guivarch1986,Bougerol1985 .}
elements.sent-2411-123: A (semi-)group $T$ acts {\em strongly irreducibly on $\R^d$ (from the right), if there is no finite union $\mathcal{W =\bigcup_{i=1 ^n W_i$ of proper subspaces $W_i\subsetneq \RR^d$, satisfying $\mathcal{W T \subset \mathcal{W}$.}}}}

elements.sent-2411-126: A matrix $a \in \GL$ is called {\em proximal , if it has an algebraically simple dominant eigenvalue, the modulus of which exceeds that of all other eigenvalues (comparable to the Perron-Frobenius eigenvalue).}
